coredata.dc:description: The rise of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) has been exponential traversing through different synthesis approaches and spread across diverse scientific and technological fields. Past decade, the application of different biological materials or systems in the synthesis of AgNPs has generated much needed impetus to drive the AgNPs synthesis through ecofriendly and economical synthesis processes. The present study aims to compare the conventional (chemical approach) and biological synthesis approach. Here in, the as-synthesized silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) via chemical (Che-AgNPs) and biological (Bio-AgNPs) methods were characterized for their optical, physicochemical, crystalline, and elemental composition properties. Che-AgNPs and Bio-AgNPs showed a peak at 455 nm and 415 nm with average particle size of 198.73 nm and 135.19 nm, whereas surface charge was found to be −21.94 mV and –32.67 mV, respectively. Che-AgNPs have shown better zone of inhibition (ZOI) against gram- bacteria such as Escherichia coli (10.57 mm), Klebsiella pneumoniae (11.25 mm), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (15.43 mm), whereas Bio-AgNPs showed better ZOI against gram + bacteria such as Bacillus subtilis (13.31 mm), Coagulase-negative staphylococci (12.28 mm), Staphylococcus aureus (12.03 mm) at 50 µg per well concentration. Che-AgNPs showed better anticancer (IC50 value in µg/ml) potential compared to Bio-AgNPs against K562 (60.60 and 109.96), HeLa (67.26 and 129.31), A549 (48.94 and 105.38) cancer cells, whereas Bio-AgNPs showed minimum toxicity towards MCF-10A normal cells. Bio-AgNPs showed better photocatalytic activity compared to Che-AgNPs against methyl red (95 % and 70 %), methylene blue (64 % and 93 %), rhodamine-B (81 % and 96 %), methyl orange (76 % and 97 %), and malachite green (62 % and 97 %) dyes under 4, 9, 7, 4, and 9 min, respectively. Furthermore, Bio-AgNPs were studied for possible involvement of reactive species and their recyclability.

head.ce:title: Chemical vs biological silver nanoparticles: Synthesis, characterization, properties, and in vitro applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, National Institute of Technology Warangal, Telangana, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology, National Institute of Technology Warangal, Telangana, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rise of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) has been exponential traversing through different synthesis approaches and spread across diverse scientific and technological fields. Past decade, the application of different biological materials or systems in the synthesis of AgNPs has generated much needed impetus to drive the AgNPs synthesis through ecofriendly and economical synthesis processes. The present study aims to compare the conventional (chemical approach) and biological synthesis approach. Here in, the as-synthesized silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) via chemical (Che-AgNPs) and biological (Bio-AgNPs) methods were characterized for their optical, physicochemical, crystalline, and elemental composition properties. Che-AgNPs and Bio-AgNPs showed a peak at 455 nm and 415 nm with average particle size of 198.73 nm and 135.19 nm, whereas surface charge was found to be −21.94 mV and –32.67 mV, respectively. Che-AgNPs have shown better zone of inhibition (ZOI) against gram- bacteria such as Escherichia coli (10.57 mm), Klebsiella pneumoniae (11.25 mm), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (15.43 mm), whereas Bio-AgNPs showed better ZOI against gram + bacteria such as Bacillus subtilis (13.31 mm), Coagulase-negative staphylococci (12.28 mm), Staphylococcus aureus (12.03 mm) at 50 µg per well concentration. Che-AgNPs showed better anticancer (IC50 value in µg/ml) potential compared to Bio-AgNPs against K562 (60.60 and 109.96), HeLa (67.26 and 129.31), A549 (48.94 and 105.38) cancer cells, whereas Bio-AgNPs showed minimum toxicity towards MCF-10A normal cells. Bio-AgNPs showed better photocatalytic activity compared to Che-AgNPs against methyl red (95 % and 70 %), methylene blue (64 % and 93 %), rhodamine-B (81 % and 96 %), methyl orange (76 % and 97 %), and malachite green (62 % and 97 %) dyes under 4, 9, 7, 4, and 9 min, respectively. Furthermore, Bio-AgNPs were studied for possible involvement of reactive species and their recyclability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metallic NPs presence has increased in several areas of human applications because of its diverse nature and potential as biosensors, solar cell technology, cryogenic superconducting material, cancer therapy, drug delivery and safety control, cosmetic industry, pollution control, pharmaceutical ingredients, antimicrobial agents, biomarkers, and primarily in biomedicine and bioscience as biological tagging agents [1–3]. Among them, AgNPs or nano-Ag has garnered a lot of attention or been in the spotlight for long due to its characteristic or unique features such as superior catalytic activity, chemical resistance, higher conductivity, superior chemical stability, enhanced magnetic, electrical, and optical properties, surface area, shape, and importantly its size [2,4,5]. These unique characteristic features of AgNPs have made them indispensable and superior compared to other metallic NPs [6]. Due to its innovative use in the energy industry, where stability and strong catalytic performance of Ag-based NPs make it a promising substitute for platinum in dye-sensitized solar cell technology, AgNPs has also received a lot of attention recently [7–9]. In current nanotech research, the synthesis of AgNPs is of essential importance in addition to its diverse applications [10]. AgNPs market size is anticipated to grow as well, from more than 2 billion USD in 2020 to more than 5.5 billion USD by 2027 [11].

The biological process for synthesizing AgNPs depends on the methodical use of plant extracts and microbes such as fungi, yeast, and bacteria which are environmentally friendly [4,12]. Microbes reduce metal ions in response to environmental stress conditions [13]. Additionally, bacteria are simple to handle, proliferate quickly and affordable, and allow for simple control of growth factors including incubation time, oxygen, and temperature [14]. Compared to physical and chemical processes, the biological synthesis of NPs is thought to be non-toxic, more affordable, and more environmentally benign [15,16]. However, a revolutionary change in the biological synthesis of NPs is envisaged by the use of extracellularly produced secondary metabolites with little interference of the toxic nature of the NPs on the biological system in order to avoid destruction of the biological resources for the synthesis of nanoparticles [14,17,18]. In addition, synthesis of NPs extracellularly is more affordable, viable, and simpler [14]. The surface of the AgNPs is covered by these biological substances, which improves their biocompatibility and stability [19]. AgNPs can have their size, shape, and morphology altered by changing the reaction conditions, such as using a reducing agent, stabilizing agent, or capping agent through various biological processes [1,4]. Furthermore, the requirement for the use of reducing agents will be eliminated if the capping agent can reduce the metal ions which produce the AgNPs [11,20,21].

The present study's utilization of marine bacterial secondary metabolites for the biological synthesis of AgNPs is predicted to provide a wide range of advantages, including the following: (a) the presence or toxicity of Ag+ ions would never affect the growth of bacteria or production (different bacteria have varying tolerances for Ag+ ions [22]), (b) NPs segregation won't go through a pointless purification process where bacteria's siderophore tends to chelate the NPs, and (c) The synthesis of NPs does not involve the use of hazardous substances [22]. For the purpose of mass-producing NPs, the same metabolite can be synthesized unimpeded from continuous or batch culturing of bacteria [22].

Low molecular weight ligands known as “siderophores” are produced as a result of the absence of soluble iron in the environment [23]. The stability constants (β) of different siderophores range from 1010 to 1049 for their iron complexation [23]. Siderophores have the potential to chelate several metal ions such as Zn2+, Tl+, Tb3+, Sn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Ga3+, Eu3+, Cu2+, Cr2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Al3+, and Ag+ apart from Fe3+ [23]. These secondary metabolites’ metal ion redox potential may be crucial for the production of metal NPs [1,23]. Bioleaching, biomineralization, and bioremediation all depend on metal-microbe interactions, or more precisely, metal-chelator interactions [23].

Therefore, based on the above stated information, the presented work focused on the comparative analysis of AgNPs synthesized through chemical and biological approaches. The objectives of the work were: (i) synthesis of AgNPs through chemical reduction (Che-AgNPs) and biological (Bio-AgNPs) (secondary metabolite (siderophore) from marine bacterial isolate [24]) approaches, (ii) physicochemical characterization of Che-AgNPs and Bio-AgNPs using various analytical techniques, (iii) to determine the antibacterial activity, (iv) to determine the anticancer potential and cyto-compatibility, (v) to determine the photocatalytic property of the as-synthesized AgNPs in the degradation of methyl red (MR), methylene blue (MB), rhodamine-B (Rh-B), methyl orange (MO) and malachite green (MG) dyes, (vi) role of reactive species in the Photocatalysis, and (vii) recyclability of the as-synthesized AgNPs.

Instruments such as Ultraviolet–Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis spec; Model: UV-1800; Manufacturer: Shimadzu; Japan), Particle size analyzer (PSA; Model: LiteSeizer 500; Manufacturer: Anton Paar, Austria), Scanning electron microscope (SEM; Tescan, Vega 3 LMU, Czech Republic) coupled with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction (XRD; Model: X’Pert Pro; Manufacturer: Malvern PANalytical, UK) were used. The as-synthesized AgNPs were separated from the solutions by centrifugation for 5 min at 15,000 rpm (RCF: 20,630xg) (KUBOTA 3300; Table-top Micro Centrifuge; RA-2024).

Bacillus subtilis (Gram + ), Coagulase-negative Staphylococci (Gram + ), Escherichia coli (Gram-), Klebsiella pneumoniae (Gram-), Proteus vulgaris (Gram-), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Gram-), and Staphylococcus aureus (Gram + ) were used for antibacterial studies. The cultures were grown using Nutrient broth and sub-cultured using Nutrient Agar plates.

The cancer cells (K562, HeLa, A549) and normal cells (MCF-10A) were regularly sub-cultured and maintained in RPMI 1640 (Lonza, Basel) media, which was supplemented with 10 % heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (Gibco, Invitrogen Life Technologies) along with antibiotics (1 % PenStrep; Sigma-Aldrich). The culture conditions were 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 static incubator. The cell culture plates were regularly monitored using inverted microscope for cell morphology, growth, and contamination. Cells were split passaged when confluence reached ∼ 80 %. Through centrifugation (3 min at 1000 rpm), cells were harvested followed by re-suspension in afore mentioned media.

Chemical synthesis: Initially, 1 mM of silver nitrate was prepared and to this 0.5 M of sodium hydroxide solution was added under continuous stirring using hot plate magnetic stirrer at 60 °C and 400 rpm for 60 min. Biological synthesis: The siderophore purified from the marine bacterial isolate [24,25] was used as reducing, capping, and stabilizing agent in the synthesis of AgNPs (Bio-AgNPs). In biological approach, both sodium hydroxide and siderophore solutions were added simultaneously under the same conditions specified for chemical synthesis. After the reaction incubation time, the solutions were allowed to cool, filtered (uisng 0.22 µm PVDF filters) and centrifuged to obtain the pellet. The obtained pellet was dried at 70 °C overnight to get AgNPs powder. The powder was then used for characteization and in vitro studies.
Characterization of the as-synthesized AgNPs was performed based on the details which were previously published by the authors without any modifications unless specified [5,16,26–28].
Antibacterial activity was determined in vitro against test bacteria via standard well diffusion method on nutrient agar plates according to the method previously published by the authors [6].

Anticancer studies of the as-synthesized AgNPs were evaluated in K562 (myelogenous leukemia), HeLa (cervical), and A549 (lung carcinoma epithelial) cancer cell lines, whereas cyto-compatibility was evaluated in MCF-10A (normal-like mammary epithelial cells) cell line by using standard MTT assay as per the previously published method [5,8]. MTT, a yellow-colored cationic tetrazolium salt permeates into the cytoplasm of the metabolically active or live cells and gets reduced by the action of mitochondrial dehydrogenase enzymes resulting in the formation of intracellular purple colored formazan precipitate or crystals. The reduction of tetrazolium salt will be toxic to cells and is considered as an endpoint to determine the viability of cells [15]. MTT assay was performed in 96-well plates.

Cell suspension (100 μl) containing ∼ 5,000 cells were transferred into the wells accordingly. Later, the plates were incubated for 24 h under the same conditions mentioned above (section 2.1.4.). After incubation, equal volume (100 μl) of freshly prepared increasing concentrations of AgNPs solution (25, 50, 75, 100, 150, and 200 μg/ml of RPMI 1640 media) were transferred into the respective wells and labeled accordingly. Then the plates were kept for incubation under the same conditions for 24 h. After incubation, MTT solution of 20 μl volume (5 mg/ml of PBS) was added to each well followed by incubation for 4 h under same conditions. This results in the formation of formazan crystals. Then, the cell suspensions were discarded without disturbing the formazan crystals or precipitate. By adding 200 μl of DMSO solvent, formazan crystals were dissolved. The absorbance of the wells was measured at 570 nm using 96-well plate reader. Well with no samples (AgNPs) was considered as negative control (blank). The obtained absorbance values were presented as mortality percentages (M%), which were calculated using Eq. (1) [15].

Where, “Vb ” and “Vs” were absorbance values observed for blank and samples at a given concentration, respectively.

Role of AgNPs as nano-catalysts in the dye degradation in presence of sunlight was studied [29]. Briefly, 5 ml of dye solution (1 mg/ml) was mixed with 10 µl of AgNPs solution (1 mg/ml) and kept under sunlight (45 to 50 ℃). The dye solution was diluted before recording the absorbance at regular intervals using UV–Vis spec. Absorbance was recorded at 554 nm, 464 nm, 664 nm, 618 nm, and 430 nm for Rh-B, MO, MB, MG, and MR dyes, respectively [6,30]. Furthermore, degradation percentage (%) (Eq. (2)) was calculated [12].

Where, ‘A0 ′ and ‘At ’ is the absorbance of dye solution at ‘0′ and ‘t’ min, respectively.

Low-molecular weight metal chelators (such as metallophores or siderophores) consist of a unique arrangement of functionally active reactive sites (such as catechols or hydroxamates) which arraign, complex, or coordinate with free metal ions, these distinctive functional associations can be used or relevant in biological context (for example, re-entry of iron-siderophore complex into bacteria) [24]. These unique microbial metallophores have evolved to complex with metal ions, unlike synthetic molecules, and because these metal complexes are crucial for metal cycle, they have specialized significance for the producing bacteria [31]. This can be seen in the acquisition of various metals, such as copper and boron, which are important within environmental niches as well as defense against particular metal toxicities [31].

The presented work emphasized on the application of siderophore (marine bacterial source [24]) in the synthesis of AgNPs through biological approach and its comparison in physicochemical properties along with in vitro potential with that of chemical synthesized AgNPs. Throughout the literature, only a few papers have highlighted the use of siderophores in the NPs synthesis (Table 1 ). The occurrence of brown color at the end of reaction time indicates the formation or synthesis of AgNPs, which is a characteristic visual indicator [4–6,10,28].

Standard analytical techniques were employed in determining the physicochemical properties of the as-synthesized AgNPs. It is of primary significance to determine the physicochemical properties of the AgNPs to understand their antibacterial, anticancer, and photocatalytic activities. Table 2 presents the results obtained from the characterization studies of the as-synthesized AgNPs (Che-AgNPs and Bio-AgNPs).

The as-synthesized AgNPs were preliminary characterized for its optical property using UV–Vis spec., which is considered as a basic technique. The Che-AgNPs showed a broader peak with λSPR at 455 nm, whereas Bio-AgNPs showed a narrow peak comparatively with λSPR at 415 nm (Fig. 1 a). The peaks observed were within the range of AgNPs as observed in the literature [4–6,10,28]. From the peak observed, it was evident that AgNPs synthesized through the biological approach have better size and shape compared to chemical approach [4,6].

Maduraimuthu et al. observed the characteristic SPR of AgNPs at 428 nm and 435 nm [32]. Shafiq et al. observed the SPR at 443 nm with a narrow peak [33]. Nejad et al. observed the occurrence of SPR in the range of 400 to 450 nm [34]. Leyu et al. observed the SPR peak at 422 nm [35]. Qanash et al. observed the sharp SPR peak at 412 nm [36].

Band Gap Energy (BGE) using Tauc’s plot is an essential parameter in determining the photocatalytic ability of the AgNPs [37]. BGE is inversely proportional to the AgNPs size [37]. If the BGE is around 3 eV they are semiconductors, if > 4 eV they are insulators [37]. The BGE for Che-AgNPs and Bio-AgNPs was found to be 3.62 and 3.09 eV, respectively (Table 2). Sumra et al. reported the BGE of 3.2 eV [38].

The PSA determined the particle size distribution of as-synthesized AgNPs to be in the range of 70 to 380 for Che-AgNPs and 60 to 230 nm for Bio-AgNPs (Table 2). The average particle size was observed to be 198.73 ± 22.56 and 135.19 ± 17.38 for Che-AgNPs and Bio-AgNPs, respectively (Fig. 1b). The poly-dispersity index (PDI) for Che-AgNPs is 0.288, whereas for Bio-AgNPs is 0.237 (Table 2). The significance of PDI value is that it defines the nature of the AgNPs colloidal solution, either polydisperse or monodisperse [32]. The PDI value will be in the range of 0 to 1, where “0″ represents the monodispersed colloidal solution and “1” represents the polydispersed colloidal solution [35].

Maduraimuthu et al. reported the PSD to be in the range of 25 to 250 nm with average particle size to be 43 nm and PDI of 1.092 [32]. Nejad et al. reported the PSD to be in the range 10 to 100 nm with average particle size to be 70.92 nm and PDI of 0.203 [34]. Leyu et al. observed the average particle size to be 68.12 nm with PDI of 0.471 whereas the PSD to be in the range of 10 to 100 nm [35]. Alfarraj et al. observed the average particle size to be 87.08 nm with PDI of 0.233 whereas the PSD to be in the range of 30 to 300 nm [39]. Mustapha et al. observed the PSD range to be in the range of 80 to 600 nm with average particle size of 186 nm and PDI to be 0.230 [40].

Surface charge determination can define the electrochemical stability of the AgNPs in the colloidal solution [35]. It can be either positive, zero, or negative charge. AgNPs with greater positive or lower negative charge offer high stability, causing repulsion between the AgNPs and avoiding aggregation [35]. The surface charge of the as-synthesized AgNPs was found to be −21.94 ± 1.68 mV for Che-AgNPs and –32.67 ± 2.27 mV for Bio-AgNPs (Fig. 1c and Table 2). It was evident that the Bio-AgNPs offers higher stability due to its lower negative charge compared to Che-AgNPs.

Maduraimuthu et al. reported the AgNPs with surface charge of −59.0 mV [32]. Nejad et al. reported the surface charge of AgNPs with −11.40 mV [34]. Leyu et al. reported the surface charge to be −25.7 mV [35]. Alfarraj et al. reported the surface charge of −14.2 for AgNPs [39]. Mustapha et al. reported the surface charge to be −39.9 mV [40].

It was evident from the XRD analysis that the as-synthesized AgNPs were crystalline in nature (Fig. 1d). Intense sharp peaks were observed at peak positions 38.18°, 44.50°, 64.28°, 77.03°, and 81.44° for both Che-AgNPs and Bio-AgNPs (Fig. 1d). Using the Debye-Scherrer’s equation, the average crystallite size “D” was found to be 23.56 nm and 18.43 nm for Che-AgNPs and Bio-AgNPs, respectively (Table 2).

Leyu et al. reported the peak positions of 38.18°, 44.25°, 64.18°, 77.38°, and 81.11° with the average crystallite size of 33.4 nm [35]. Maduraimuthu et al. reported the peak positions of 38.34°, 44.91°, 65.29°, and 77.47° [32]. Shafiq et al. reported the occurrence of peak positions of 37.91°, 44.08°, 64.3°, and 77.30° with average crystallite size to be 26.82 nm [33]. Rehman et al. reported the peak positions of 27.71°, 32.15°, 38.13°, 44.3°, 46.11°, 54.63°, 57.48°, 64.35°, and 77.40° [41]. Afandy et al. reported the peak positions of 38°, 44°, and 64° with average crystallite size to be 28.1 nm [42].

SEM and EDX analysis were performed to determine the morphology and elemental composition of the as-synthesized AgNPs. From the results it was evident that the NPs synthesized were indeed AgNPs (Fig. 1g and 1 h). Che-AgNPs showed about 94.9 % of Ag in weight percentage (Wt.%), whereas Bio-AgNPs showed about 87.4 % of Ag (Table 2).

Maduraimuthu et al. reported the occurrence of peaks in the range of 2.7 to 3.5 KeV in EDX spectra [32]. Shafiq et al. reported a strong EDX peak at 3 KeV with wt.% of 88.4 for Ag [33]. Qanash et al. reported the presence of strong EDX peak at 3 KeV with wt.% of 76.67 for Ag [36]. Mustapha et al. reported a sharp peak at 3 KeV in EDX spectra with wt.% of ∼ 45 for Ag [40]. Sumra et al. reported the presence of three sharp peaks in EDX spectra at 0.8, 2.7, and 3 KeV [38].

Since the dawn of time, people have been aware of the antibacterial characteristics of noble metals [19]. Ag compounds, metallic Ag, and Ag salts have been used to successfully stop microbial development since the beginning of time [19]. AgNPs, which have outstanding antibacterial effects against a variety of microbial diseases due to their enormous surface area to volume ratio, were made possible by advances in nanotechnology [43].

Gram + bacteria have thick cell walls usually ranging from 20 to 80 nm, which is made up of only one but thick peptidoglycan layer consisting of teichoic acid [44,45]. On the contrary, gram- bacteria have comparatively thin cell walls usually ranging from 8 to 10 nm, which is made up of thin phospholipid layers (typically more than one) sandwiching the peptidoglycan layer without the teichoic acid [44,45]. The surface of both groups has a negative charge, however the gram- bacteria show high negative charge compared to gram + bacteria [46]. The interaction between the bacterial cell wall and the NPs is governed by their surface charge [47]. It was also observed that the lipopolysaccharide structure has great impact over the binding of NPs to the lipid bilayer in gram- bacteria [48]. On the other hand, gram + bacteria lack lipid bilayer or lipopolysaccharide layer and along with lower negative charge can be more susceptible to NPs permeability [45,46].

Bio-AgNPs with high-negative overall surface charge (–32.67 mV) might be repelled by the high-negative surface charge of the gram- bacteria. Whereas the gram + bacteria have comparatively lower charge and are more susceptible to the NPs with higher-negative charge, even though they have thick peptidoglycan layer. On the other hand, Che-AgNPs (−21.9) had lower-negative charge compared to Bio-AgNPs, therefore they have shown good activity against gram- bacteria. Even though the size of Bio-AgNPs (135.19 nm) is smaller compared to Che-AgNPs (198.73), the overall surface charge (i.e., high-negative) might have played a crucial role or influenced the binding of Che-AgNPs to the surface of gram- bacteria. Therefore, it was noted that along with size, surface charge is equal or in some cases more important to exhibit good antibacterial activity [45,46].

From Fig. 2o to 2u and Table 2, it was also observed that the as-synthesized AgNPs showed highest ZOI at 50 μg per well concentration against test bacteria. From Fig. 2a to 2n, it was noticed that the AgNPs were prone to aggregation as the concentration increased beyond 50 μg per well. In comparison, Che-AgNPs has shown more aggregation, which can be seen by the occurrence of dark brown color ring formation at the well edges. Bio-AgNPs were less prone to aggregation might be because of their higher negative surface charge (Fig. 2c and Table 2). Among the test bacteria, only P. aeruginosa has shown concentration dependent ZOI (Fig. 2t).

AgNPs and the Ag+ ions produced when they dissolve both have antibacterial properties. AgNPs and Ag+ ions can both interact with various bacterial cells and biofilm constituents. They hinder bacterial metabolism and outside cellular processes through these interactions [49]. Overall, the antibacterial potential of AgNPs is due to the combination of several mechanisms such as generation of reactive oxygen species eliciting oxidative stress, nucleic acids damage, hindering electron transport chain, inactivation of relevant enzymes, destabilization of protein structures, and destruction of cell membrane [4,12,15]. Ag NPs are now a viable option for antimicrobial therapy as a result of its complex antibacterial mechanism [50].

The anticancer potential of the as-synthesized AgNPs (Che-AgNPs and Bio-AgNPs) was evaluated in three different cancer cell lines (K562, HeLa, and A549). Both the AgNPs showed concentration dependent activity against the three tested cancer cell lines (Table 4 ). From the results (Fig. 3 and Table 4), it was also evident that the Che-AgNPs showed better activity compared to Bio-AgNPs against K562 (Fig. 3a), HeLa (Fig. 3b), and A549 (Fig. 3c) cancer cell lines. Table 5 presents the comparison of IC50 value of the as-synthesized AgNPs (Che-AgNPs and Bio-AgNPs) with that of reported in literature.

The as-synthesized AgNPs (Che-AgNPs and Bio-AgNPs) were studied for their cyto-compatibility using MCF-10A normal cells. From Fig. 3d and Table 4, it was evident that the Bio-AgNPs showed less toxicity compared to Che-AgNPs. The IC50 (μg/ml) value of Che-AgNPs was found to be at 34.99 ± 1.29, whereas for Bio-AgNPs it was > 200. Thus, Bio-AgNPs are more compatible compared to Che-AgNPs. This is possible due to the involvement of siderophore in the synthesis of Bio-AgNPs.

Kumkoon et al. reported the use of MCF-10A normal mammary epithelial cells to evaluate the cyto-compatibility of AgNPs, which showed no toxicity in the concentration range of 0.1 to 50 μg/ml [51]. Ali et al. reported no toxicity against MCF-10A cells in the concentration range of 6.25 to 100 μg/ml even after 72 h exposure [52]. Jayeoye et al. reported the biocompatibility studies of AgNPs using L929 mouse fibroblast cells, which showed 62.21 % cell viability at highest test concentration (110 μg/ml) [53]. Lozano-Pérez et al. also reported the use of L929 mouse fibroblast cells to evaluate the cyto-compatibility of AgNPs, which showed 30 % higher cell viability at 10 μg/ml concentration [54]. John et al. reported the use of Human Adult dermal fibroblasts cells to evaluate the cyto-compatibility of AgNPs, which showed 99.82 % cell viability even at 10 μg/ml concentration [55].

The photocatalytic activity of as-synthesized AgNPs was studied both in the presence and absence of AgNPs and sunlight, separately. In cases where AgNPs and sunlight were absent, degradation of dyes was not observed. Also, in the presence of only AgNPs or sunlight did not show any degradation or change in the color intensity. The degradation was only observed in presence of both AgNPs and sunlight (Fig. 4 a). From the results (Fig. 4b and Table 6 ), it was evident that Bio-AgNPs showed better photocatalytic activity compared to Che-AgNPs. This is due to the smaller crystallite size and lower overall surface charge of the Bio-AgNPs compared to the Che-AgNPs (Table 2). Table 7 presents the comparison of the Bio-AgNPs photocatalytic activity in the degradation of MR, MB, Rh-B, MO, and MG with those reported in literature. Fig. 4c presents the schematic representation of possible mechanisms involved in the degradation of dyes in presence of both AgNPs and sunlight [56,57]. Further studies, such as the effect of scavengers and recyclability were studied for only Bio-AgNPs.

The effect of scavengers was studied to determine the possible involvement of reactive species (such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide radicals (•O2 –), holes (h+), and electrons (e-)) in the photocatalytic degradation of MR, MB, Rh-B, MO, and MG dyes in presence of Bio-AgNPs. Chemicals such as isopropyl alcohol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (BQ), ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA), and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were used at a concentration of 1 mM to quench •OH, •O2 –, h+, and e- reactive species, respectively. Furthermore, effect of scavengers in combination was also determined. From the results (Fig. 5 and Table 8 ), it was clear that the degradation was achieved through the involvement of various reactive species for different dyes. Degradation reaction with no scavengers was considered as control.

For MR, presence of IPA (•OH) and BQ (•O2 –) has reduced the degradation to 47.43 % and 57.22 %, respectively (Fig. 5a). Whereas combined effect of IPA and BQ showed only 10.21 % degradation, highlighting the involvement of both •OH and •O2 – reactive species in the degradation of MR. For MB, presence of BQ (•O2 –) and EDTA (h+) has reduced the degradation to 43.88 % and 62.43 %, respectively (Fig. 5b). Whereas combined effect of BQ and EDTA showed only 17.85 % degradation, highlighting the involvement of both •O2 – and h+ reactive species in the degradation of MB. For Rh-B, presence of EDTA (h+) and AgNO3 (e-) has reduced the degradation to 63.68 % and 63.49 %, respectively (Fig. 5c). Whereas combined effect of EDTA and AgNO3 showed only 34.94 % degradation, highlighting the involvement of both h+ and e- reactive species in the degradation of Rh-B. For MO, presence of EDTA (h+) and AgNO3 (e-) has reduced the degradation to 67.53 % and 48.55 %, respectively (Fig. 5d). Whereas combined effect of EDTA and AgNO3 showed only 21.75 % degradation, highlighting the involvement of both h+ and e- reactive species in the degradation of MO. For MG, presence of IPA (•OH) and AgNO3 (e-) has reduced the degradation to 57.07 % and 71.83 %, respectively (Fig. 5e). Whereas combined effect of IPA and AgNO3 showed only 34.91 % degradation, highlighting the involvement of both •OH and e- reactive species in the degradation of MG. Table 9 presents the comparison of Bio-AgNPs photocatalytic activity in the presence of various scavengers with those reported in literature.

The recyclability of Bio-AgNPs studied to determine the possible reusability. From the results (Fig. 6 ), it was evident that the Bio-AgNPs showed moderate to good recyclability against the tested textile dyes. Table 10 presents the recyclability details obtained during the degradation of MR, MB, Rh-B, MO, and MG dyes. Table 11 presents the comparison of the Bio-AgNPs recyclability with those reported in literature.

The as-synthesized AgNPs were characterized for their physicochemical properties using standard analytical techniques. Furthermore, the evaluation of their antibacterial property determined surface charge dependent activity. Che-AgNPs showed better activity against gram- bacteria, whereas Bio-AgNPs showed better activity against gram + bacteria. Based on anticancer studies, Che-AgNPs were found to be more potent compared to Bio-AgNPs against the test cell lines. Similarly, against MCF-10A (normal cell line), Bio-AgNPs showed less toxicity compared to Che-AgNPs, highlighting the possible role of siderophore in offering the biocompatibility. Photocatalytic studies showed that Bio-AgNPs were better compared to Che-AgNPs. Additionally, the effect of scavengers revealed the major reactive species involved in the degradation of MR, MB, Rh-B, MO, and MG dyes. Recyclability studies of Bio-AgNPs showed moderate to good reusability, which is an important trait for the possible on field application. Overall, the Bio-AgNPs showed better results compared to Che-AgNPs in terms of cyto-compatibility and photocatalytic studies.

Mounika Sarvepalli: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Aditya Velidandi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Narasimhulu Korrapati: Supervision.
